summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that the ragas library fetches attacker-controlled URLs via urllib.request.urlopen() without scheme validation, private IP blocking, or response validation. A local HTTP server receives a request when a URL with an image extension is included in the prompt value items.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction pipeline should cause the ragas library to fetch an internal URL (http://127.0.0.1:18088/internal_secret.png) via urllib.request.urlopen(), confirming the SSRF vulnerability. The local HTTP server should receive the request and the proof token should be printed.
actual_observed_result: Running the exploit script started a local HTTP server on 127.0.0.1:18088, created an ImageTextPromptValue with a URL pointing to the local server, called to_messages() which triggered download_and_encode_image(), and the server received the GET request. The PROOF_TOKEN was printed, confirming the SSRF.
verification_method: The exploit script starts a local HTTP server, provides a URL to the server in the prompt value, and checks whether the server received a request. If the server received the request, the SSRF is confirmed. The proof token (EVOHUNT_SSRF_PROOF_7f3a9b2c) distinguishes a real SSRF from unrelated network activity.
evidence_produced_by_run: The local HTTP server received a GET request for /internal_secret.png
The response was base64-encoded by download_and_encode_image(), confirming the response content would be included in the LLM prompt
The PROOF_TOKEN EVOHUNT_SSRF_PROOF_7f3a9b2c was printed, confirming the SSRF was triggered through the vulnerable code path

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable commit, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
